coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots@Fe3O4 and their photocatalytic degradation properties to hexaconazole
coredata.dc:creator: Rong, Shuang
Tang, Xinyi
Liu, Huihui
Xu, Junhu
Yuan, Zitong
Peng, Xinya
Niu, Jinzhi
Wu, Yan
He, Lin
Qian, Kun
coredata.dc:description: The CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis method. The CD@Fe3O4 particles were synthesized using Fe3O4 as the core and using citric acid and ethylenediamine as a raw material, which were heated to 200 °C for 4 h. The synthesized fluorescent CD@Fe3O4 was characterized by HR-TEM, IR and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The HR-TEM results showed CD and Fe3O4 nanoparticles were uniform, mono-dispersed sphere or hemisphere particles with an average size of approximately 3 nm, and particle size of CD@Fe3O4 were mainly in range of 20–30 nm. XRD results showed the nanoparticles mainly belonged to Fe3O4 and CD@Fe3O4, which made recycling our photocatalysts possible due to the magnetic performance. On daylight lamp, the half-life of hexaconazole in CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts was about 4 days, and it is lower than half-life (over 100 days) of hexaconazole without CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CD@Fe3O4
Hexaconazole
Photocatalytic degradation

Effects of different amounts of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts on degradation rate of hexaconazole
Effects of different pH values on degradation rate of hexaconazole

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of CD (a), Fe3O4 (b), CD@Fe3O4 (c), and size distribution of CD@Fe3O4(d).
Fluorescence curves of CD and CD@Fe3O4.
XRD spectrum of Fe3O4 and CD@Fe3O4.
The effects of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts on the photocatalytic efficiency of hexaconazole aqueous solution.
The effects of different concentration of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts on the photocatalytic efficiency of hexaconazole aqueous solution.
The effects of different concentration of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts on the photocatalytic efficiency of hexaconazole aqueous solution in different pH condition.
The recovery and precision of hexaconazole in water.
Effects of different conditions on half-life of hexaconazole in daylight.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots@Fe3O4 and their photocatalytic degradation properties to hexaconazole

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Plant Protection, Southwest University, Chongqing 400716, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Plant Protection, Southwest University, Chongqing 400716, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400716, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400716, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Southwest University, No. 2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis method. The CD@Fe3O4 particles were synthesized using Fe3O4 as the core and using citric acid and ethylenediamine as a raw material, which were heated to 200 °C for 4 h. The synthesized fluorescent CD@Fe3O4 was characterized by HR-TEM, IR and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The HR-TEM results showed CD and Fe3O4 nanoparticles were uniform, mono-dispersed sphere or hemisphere particles with an average size of approximately 3 nm, and particle size of CD@Fe3O4 were mainly in range of 20–30 nm. XRD results showed the nanoparticles mainly belonged to Fe3O4 and CD@Fe3O4, which made recycling our photocatalysts possible due to the magnetic performance. On daylight lamp, the half-life of hexaconazole in CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts was about 4 days, and it is lower than half-life (over 100 days) of hexaconazole without CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the use of pesticides contributes to mass and stable production of the agriculture. However, pesticides can have an adverse effect on the environment in addition to being harmful to humans, animals and fishes (Liang et al., 2012). Pesticide residue has been an important issue for human health, environment and food safety (Lan et al., 2016; Casado et al., 2018). Significantly, it was important to decrease the level of pesticide residue. Photocatalytic oxidation using photocatalyst is an effective way to reduce pesticide residue in the environment. Recently, photocatalysis has been extensively investigated due to its advantages such as non-toxicity, safety, good stability, high catalytic activity and lower energy consumption (Padmapriya et al., 2016). In the photocatalytic system, nano-metal semiconductor has been widely used in the degradation of organic dye pollutants, toxic gases and pesticides (Han et al., 2014).

Recently, the incorporation of magnetic components into composite photocatalyst attracted significant attention (Wang et al., 2014). Xuan et al. (2008) prepared hollow spherical Fe3O4/TiO2 hybrid photocatalysts. Which exhibited good photocatalytic activity under UV light irradiation and can be recycled by magnetic separation. Xu and Wang (2012) prepared magnetic nanoscaled Fe3O4/CeO2 composite by the impregnation method, which showed a promising application of the catalyst in the oxidative degradation of organic pollutants. Xi et al. (2011) had synthesized a magnetically recyclable Fe3O4/WO3 core–shell visible-light photocatalyst and it was significantly enhanced the photodegradation activity of organic dye materials.

Hexaconazole is a fungicide, which is widely used to protect many fungi, particularly Ascomycetes and Basidiomycetes (Wang et al., 2005). Due to its wide application, a large number of hexaconazole residues remained in the environment, which threated the environment and food safety (Liang et al., 2012).

Currently, many studies have been performed to investigate the efficacy, chiral structure and toxicity of hexazolidazole (Zhang et al., 2012). However, few studies were done to investigate the photocatalytic degradation of hexazolidazole. Ren et al. (2004) showed that hexaconazole was difficult to degrade in natural light, which also demonstrated that the photochemical degradation rate of hexaconazole was different under different solvents, and the degradation efficiency was different under ultraviolet light under the condition of hydrogen peroxide. In addition to, a number of studies have shown that the photocatalytic degradation of hexaconazole using catalytic materials requires the addition of oxidants (Shanmugam et al., 2016). Nowadays, photocatalytic degradation technology in the pesticide is not yet mature (Hu et al., 2014; Calza et al., 2008). Calza et al. (2008) studied that there were not relative researches about degradation of hexaconazole in daylight. We here report a strategy to degrade it, which based on photocatalytic degradation.

Many studies confirmed that the traditional quantum dots have strong optical response in the visible region, adjustable optical properties and controllable band width. Furthermore, the synthesis process of carbon quantum dots is convenient, non-toxic and stable photoluminescence properties which had more advantages in the practical application (Wang et al., 2015). Meanwhile, some studies have shown that carbon dots (CD) could modify metal oxides (Fe2O3), broaden the absorption range of metal oxides and enhance the photocatalytic degradation activity of organic pollutants under visible light. However, there did not have any researches on degradation of chemical pesticide with recycled magnetic CD@Fe3O4.

In this study, the nanoscale CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts were synthesized via simple steps. The effect of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts and Fe3O4 photocatalysts on hexaconazole degradation rate in the daylight were studied.

Hexaconazole standard was provided by environmental protection research and Testing Institute of Ministry of agriculture (Tianjin, China). Citric acid, ferric chloride, ferrous chloride, oleic acid and ethanol were purchased from Chengdu clonal Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). Acetone was purchased from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). Ethylenediamine (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Concentrated ammonia (25%) was purchased from Shenzhen zhixinhai Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Acetonitrile were HPLC grade and purchased from Thermo Fisher Technology (China) Co., Ltd.

The Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were prepared through coprecipitation method as previously reported (Ghafuri et al., 2016). Briefly, 8.1 g of FeCl3 6H2O were dissolved in appropriate of deionized water. The obtained solution was transferred to a three-necked flask and heated to 70 °C with stirring. 4.4 g of FeCl2 4H2O dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, filtered, and took 75 mL added to the three-necked flask. Under vigorous stirring, 18 mL of 25% concentrated ammonia was added. After 1 min, 4.66 g of oleinic acid was added dropwise, and the mixture was stirred at 70 °C for 1 h. After completion of the reaction, the black jelly was obtained, and the resulting precipitate was separated from the reaction system by an external magnetic field. The obtained mixture was washed with ethanol several times to remove the excess oleinic acid and deionized water to neutral.

0.1 g of obtained Fe3O4 particles and 10 g citric acid were dispersed into 100 mL of deioned water in a three-necked flask and heated to 70 °C with stirring. After complete dissolution, 3.5 mL of ethylenediamine was added and heated to 200 °C. After 5 h a dark green liquid was obtained. The product was purified with acetone and lyophilized to get CD@Fe3O4 (Jiang et al., 2016).
The particle size, and morphology analysis of nanoparticles are characterized by DLS (Zetasizer Nano ZS90) and HR-TEM (Zeiss Libra 200), respectively.
The crystal structures of nanoparticles were investigated using a Philips TMX-ray diffractometer.

The photocatalytic activities of CD@Fe3O4 were evaluated by degradation of hexaconazole under visible light irradiation. Hexaconazole solution (1000 mg/L) were prepared in acetonitrile, protected from light and stored at 4 °C. The degradation of water suspension system, prepared with amount of catalysts and 50 mL of hexaconazole solution in 10 mg/L concentrations. The water suspension system put in 150 mL of triangular bottle. Hexadiazolole was diluted in 5% acetonitrile aqueous solution. A 20 W Philips daylight lamp was used for visible light irradiation. The suspension system was positioned about 10 cm from daylight lamp. The water samples were collected 10 days and the first day collected in 6 h, 12 h, 24 h.

Linearity of the method was evaluated over the range of 0.50–10 mg/L using blank carbon dots solution spiked with working standard hexaconazole. Calibration samples were prepared as described earlier. Linear calibration curves were obtained by plotting peak areas versus the concentration of each pesticide, and liner regression analysis was performed using Microsoft Excel® (Liang et al., 2012).

Precision and accuracy of the assay were obtained by comparing the predicted concentration (obtained from the calibration curve) to the actual concentration of each pesticide spiked in blank carbon dots solution. Standard deviation (SD) and relative standard deviation (RSD = SD/mean) were calculated over the entire calibration range. The recovery was calculated from the ratio of the peak area of each sample after extraction to the peak area of an equivalent amount of the standard solution (Muhamad et al., 2012).

Limit of detection (LOD) for each sample was considered to be the concentration that produced a signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3. Limit of quantification (LOQ) was defined as the lowest concentration in the calibration curve with acceptable precision and accuracy (The acceptance criteria for the LOQ were that the precision and accuracy for extracted samples were under 20% variability).

The concentration of hexaconazole were determined under reverse phase HPLC condition by an Agilent 1260 system. The signal was collected by an Agilent Chemstation. Hexaconazole was analyzed on a Kormasil C18 column (250 mm × 4.6 mm (i. d.), 5 μm). The mobile phase was a mixture of 60% acetonitrile and 40% water with a flow rate of 0.8 mL/min. Chromatographic separation was conducted at a column temperature of 20 °C with UV detection at 210 nm (Kuriakose et al., 2014).

The degradation of hexaconazole with CD@Fe3O4 appeared to follow a pseudo-first-order kinetic reaction, and the degradation rate constants were determined by Eq. (1):
(1)
k =
ln
C /
C 0
/ t
where C 0 is initial concentration of enantiomer (μM), C is its concentration (μM) at time t (min), and k is degradation rate constant (Wang et al., 2014).The degradation half-life (t 1/2) was determined by Eq. (2):
(2)
t
1 / 2
= 0.693 / k

The results of the recovery and precision study are shown in Table 1 . The accuracy and precision of the assay, ranged from 0.70 to 3.28% (RSD) and 86–90.6% (accuracy) over the entire calibration range. The value of LOD was 0.02 mg/L and LOQ was 0.05 mg/L (Wang et al., 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of carbon dots
Effects of Fe3O4 and CD@Fe3O4 on degradation rate of hexaconazole
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts on the photocatalytic efficiency of hexaconazole aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 4 . For the hexaconazole aqueous solution treated with CD, degradation rate was zero similar with control until 10th day (Jiang et al., 2016). Fe3O4 could speed up the degradation of hexaconazole about 15% at the 6th day, while the degradation of hexaconazole was about 50% with CD@Fe3O4. The t1/2 of hexaconazole with Fe3O4 and CD@Fe3O4 were 34.8 and 6.2 day, which showed CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts speed up degradation of hexaconazole in the daylight (Muhamad et al., 2012). Effects of different conditions on half-life of hexaconazole in daylight was showed in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different amounts of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts on degradation rate of hexaconazole
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different concentration of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts on the photocatalytic efficiency of hexaconazole aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 5 . For the CD@Fe3O4，degradation rate of hexaconazole increased gradually from 0.25 g to 1.5 g. For 0.25 g CD@Fe3O4, degradation of hexaconazole was about 30% at the 6th day, while for 1.5 g, the degradation of hexaconazole was about 65% with CD@Fe3O4 at the 6th day. Through the degradation kinetics curve, we can know that degradation of hexaconazole accorded with first order kinetic curve. Half-life period of hexaconazole from 0.25 to 1.5 g CD@Fe3O4 became from 12.5 to 3.68 days. From 0.25 to 0.5 g, half-life period reduced about 50%, which showed the highest degradation efficiency (Lakshmanan et al., 2007; Chauhan et al., 2017). As the increase of the photocatalysts concentration, the surface area of the composite and the active sites in the aqueous solution increase accordingly, and the higher light utilization improve photocatalytic degradation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different pH values on degradation rate of hexaconazole
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different concentration of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts on the photocatalytic efficiency of hexaconazole aqueous solution in different pH condition are shown in Fig. 6 for the hexaconazole aqueous solution treated with different pH from 5 to 9. The degradation of hexaconazole was about 50% with CD@Fe3O4 in the neutral condition at the 6th day. In the acidic and alkaline condition, the degradation of hexaconazole was slightly lower, which might be ascribed to higher efficiency in the neutral condition. Through the degradation kinetics curve, we could know that degradation of hexaconazole accord with first order kinetic curve. Half-life period of hexaconazole treated with CD@Fe3O4 were 6.5, 5.9, 6.7 with pH 5, 7, 9 respectively.

In this paper, through preparation of CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts, half-life of hexaconazole was decreased to 4 days on daylight lamp. In comparison, half-life of hexaconazole was over 100 days without CD@Fe3O4 photocatalysts. At the same time, the recycling properties of our photocatalysts possible contributed to the magnetic performance of composed Fe3O4. That provided a new kind of catalytic material of hexaconazole in daylight, which also could be used in degradation of other pesticide in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China-Major International (Regional) Joint Research Project (32020103010) and The National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFD020030106, 2016YFD0200500).
